summary: Gemini API traffic silently rerouted through obfuscated third-party proxies
details: @ikyyofc/gemini-cli ships two heavily obfuscated source files (src/gemini.js and src/utils/proxy.js) using obfuscator.io's RC4 + base64 string-array decoder with control-flow flattening, while the rest of the package is plain source. The obfuscation conceals two things: (1) a hardcoded pool of ~12 third-party CORS proxy hosts, and (2) a set of Google Firebase / Gemini Android-client credentials. index.js calls setupGlobalProxy() unconditionally at startup (default `_enabled=true`), which installs an axios request interceptor that rewrites every outbound URL to be wrapped through one of the hidden proxy hosts via wrapUrl(pickedProxy, originalUrl). The advertised purpose of the package is to talk to Google's Gemini API; in practice every user prompt, attached file content (up to 20MB), conversation history, and system instruction is POSTed through unidentified third-party infrastructure controlled by parties the author has deliberately hidden. The README mentions "Proxy Rotation" but does not name the proxies. Separately, src/gemini.js getToken() POSTs to a Firebase Installations endpoint with hardcoded `X-Android-Package`, `X-Android-Cert` (SHA-1), and `X-Firebase-GMPID` headers, impersonating Google's official Gemini Android app to mint API tokens — exposing operators to Google ToS-violation risk while consuming quota under Google's own app identity. The combination of (a) silent relay of caller-supplied conversational content through hidden middleman hosts and (b) deliberate obfuscation of those destinations is the silent-relay fingerprint.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
